content:
# Matthew Chapter 13 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_013_page_001.jpg, MAT_013_page_002.jpg, MAT_013_page_003.jpg

### The Parable of the Sower

1. That same day, Jesus left the house and sat down by the lake.

2. Such a huge crowd gathered around him that he got into a boat and sat there while the entire crowd stood along the shore.

3. He taught them many things through stories. "A farmer went out to plant his seed," he began.

4. "As he scattered it, some seed fell on the footpath, and the birds came and ate it.

5. Other seed fell on shallow soil with rock underneath. It sprouted quickly because the soil was thin,

6. but when the sun came up, the plants were scorched and withered because they had no deep roots.

7. Other seed fell among thorns, which grew up and choked the young plants.

8. But some seed fell on rich soil and produced a crop — a hundred, sixty, or thirty times what was planted.

9. Anyone with ears to hear should listen!"

### Why Jesus Used Parables

10. His disciples came and asked him, "Why do you use parables when you talk to the people?"

11. He replied, "You've been given the privilege of understanding the secrets of the kingdom of heaven, but they haven't.

12. Whoever has understanding will be given more, and they'll have plenty. But whoever lacks understanding — even what little they have will be taken from them.

13. That's why I use parables with these people: they look but don't really see; they listen but don't really hear or understand.

14. Isaiah's prophecy is being fulfilled in them: 'You will listen and listen but never understand; you will look and look but never see.

15. For the hearts of this people have become calloused; their ears are hard of hearing, and they've shut their eyes — otherwise they might see, hear, understand, turn around, and I would heal them.'

16. But how fortunate you are — your eyes see and your ears hear!

17. I tell you the truth, many prophets and righteous people longed to see what you're seeing and didn't see it, and to hear what you're hearing and didn't hear it.

### Jesus Explains the Sower

18. So listen to what the parable of the farmer means.

19. The seed that fell on the footpath represents those who hear the message about the kingdom but don't understand it. The evil one comes and snatches away what was planted in their hearts.

20. The seed on the rocky soil represents those who hear the message and immediately receive it with excitement.

21. But they have no depth. They last only a short time — when trouble or persecution comes because of the message, they quickly fall away.

22. The seed among the thorns represents those who hear the message, but the worries of life and the seduction of wealth crowd it out, and no fruit is produced.

23. But the seed on the rich soil represents those who hear the message and truly grasp it. They produce an enormous crop — a hundred, sixty, or thirty times what was planted."

### The Parable of the Weeds

24. Jesus told them another parable: "The kingdom of heaven is like a farmer who planted good seed in his field.

25. But while everyone was sleeping, his enemy came and scattered weed seeds among the wheat and slipped away.

26. When the wheat sprouted and formed heads of grain, the weeds appeared too.

27. The farmer's workers came to him and said, 'Sir, didn't you plant good seed in your field? Where did all these weeds come from?'

28. 'An enemy did this,' he replied. The workers asked, 'Should we go pull them up?'

29. 'No,' he answered, 'because while you're pulling up the weeds, you might uproot the wheat with them.

30. Let them both grow together until harvest. Then I'll tell the harvesters: First collect the weeds and tie them in bundles to be burned, then gather the wheat into my barn.'"

### Mustard Seed and Yeast

31. He told them another parable: "The kingdom of heaven is like a mustard seed that someone planted in a field.

32. It's the smallest of all seeds, but when it grows, it becomes the largest of garden plants — it turns into a tree big enough for birds to come and nest in its branches."

33. He told them yet another parable: "The kingdom of heaven is like yeast that a woman mixed into a large amount of flour until it worked all the way through the dough."

34. Jesus always used parables when speaking to the crowds. He never spoke to them without using parables.

35. This fulfilled what the prophet had said: "I will speak in parables; I will explain mysteries hidden since the creation of the world."

### Jesus Explains the Weeds

36. Then Jesus left the crowd and went into the house. His disciples came to him and said, "Explain the parable of the weeds in the field."

37. He answered, "The farmer who plants the good seed is the Son of Man.

38. The field is the world. The good seed represents the people of the kingdom. The weeds represent the people of the evil one.

39. The enemy who planted the weeds is the devil. The harvest is the end of the age. The harvesters are angels.

40. Just as the weeds are pulled up and burned, that's how it will be at the end of the age.

41. The Son of Man will send out his angels, and they will remove from his kingdom everything that causes sin and everyone who does evil.

42. They will throw them into the blazing furnace, where there will be weeping and grinding of teeth.

43. Then the righteous will shine like the sun in their Father's kingdom. Anyone with ears to hear should listen!

### Hidden Treasure, Pearl, and Net

44. The kingdom of heaven is like a treasure buried in a field. When a man discovered it, he hid it again and was so thrilled that he went and sold everything he owned to buy that field.

45. Again, the kingdom of heaven is like a jewel merchant searching for fine pearls.

46. When he found one of extraordinary value, he went and sold everything he had and bought it.

47. Once more — the kingdom of heaven is like a fishing net cast into the sea that catches all kinds of fish.

48. When it's full, the fishermen haul it to shore, sit down, and sort the catch — the good fish into baskets, the worthless ones thrown away.

49. That's how it will be at the end of the age. The angels will come and separate the wicked from the righteous,

50. throwing the wicked into the blazing furnace, where there will be weeping and grinding of teeth."

### New and Old Treasures

51. "Do you understand all this?" Jesus asked. "Yes," they replied.

52. Then he said, "Every teacher of the law who has become a student of the kingdom of heaven is like a homeowner who brings out of his storeroom treasures both new and old."

### Rejected in His Hometown

53. When Jesus had finished these parables, he moved on from there.

54. He went to his hometown and began teaching in their synagogue. The people were stunned. "Where did he get this wisdom and these miraculous powers?" they asked.

55. "Isn't this the carpenter's son? Isn't his mother Mary? Aren't James, Joseph, Simon, and Judas his brothers?

56. And aren't all his sisters living right here among us? So where does he get all this?"

57. And they were deeply offended by him. But Jesus told them, "A prophet is honored everywhere except in his hometown and his own family."

58. And he didn't do many miracles there because of their lack of faith.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 11 — "secrets of the kingdom":** The Greek μυστήρια doesn't mean "puzzles." It means truths that were once hidden but are now being revealed. The parables are not riddles to confuse — they're keys to unlock reality for those ready to receive them.

**Verse 12 — "whoever has... whoever lacks":** This sounds harsh, but it describes a spiritual dynamic: engagement with truth produces more understanding; indifference produces atrophy. It's not punishment — it's the natural consequence of response or refusal.

**Verses 24-30 — The weeds parable:** The weeds (ζιζάνια) are darnel — a plant that looks exactly like wheat until harvest time. The point: God's kingdom coexists with evil in the world, and premature attempts to purge evil will damage the good. Judgment belongs to harvest time, not the growing season.

**Verse 33 — "yeast":** In most biblical contexts, yeast represents corruption (see 16:6). Here Jesus subverts expectations: the kingdom works like yeast — small, hidden, but permeating everything. Three measures of flour is about 40 pounds — enough bread for over a hundred people. The kingdom starts tiny but transforms everything.

**Verse 44 — "sold everything":** Both the treasure and the pearl parables emphasize the same thing: the kingdom is worth total investment. The joy is the key — the man doesn't sacrifice reluctantly. He sells everything gladly because of what he's found.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
